title: Offline Mode property / param in settings. Limit of ellevations, connections, socketing and fs reading overall.
description:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ⚠️⚠️ -->

//  Enable offline Mode in VSCIDE

<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->

<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->


<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
While this is doable / possible after running many OS restrictions, network restrictions and akins, most productive environments can't coope with all that is necessary to ensure they can safely run VsCode without ending up pinging microsoft, sharing buffers and IPC sockets, unrestricted in terms of anything that is not "execution permissions at filesystem / kernel level.".

Enable a way to run totally offline, disconnected from any feedback, ping, or socket hook.
Ideally even opt out of some kernel comunication, limit fs integration and etc.
Don't open agents, communications or any sybling proccess while running this.

Reasoning and goals :

Avoiding mapping and sharing anything when running privacy critical mode, and enable running in a more "restricted" environment that may work akin to Don't trust project, but related to NetworkINtegration and Socket.

If this is analyzed and intended to be developed, I'm open to help implement the effort.
comments: Running without extensions enabled and with updates turned off should get you pretty close.  If you have any specific asks that aren't solved after configuring these two options, please share.

title: feature request: add function like `languages.match()` for `Uri` in extension API
description:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
For extension authoring, I request **a function to test whether a language identifier or a glob pattern matches a file path (`Uri` or `string`)** in the extension API.

According to [the official document about glob patterns](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editor/glob-patterns), VS code has its own pattern matcher. I think `languages.match()` uses this but it is for `TextDocument`, i.e., a file open in the editor. I think there are cases where extensions want to know the language of file not open in an editor.

## Scenario this function is required

* A extension can handle files in two or more different format using different language identifiers, e.g., `"{ *.foo": "foo-lang"}` and `{*.bar": "bar-lang"}`. 
* The extension can show these kind of files in a webview or so, without a document editor.
* The extension wants to implement for context menu in explorer view for opening a file in a webview, for example. `when` clause in package.json limits the command (e.g., `"when": "resourceLangId =~ /^(foo|bar)-lang$/"`
* A command handler receives the file URI. Then it wants to know which languages the URI is to use appropriate parser. User may have customized the `files.associations` setting and so, just comparing the file extension with the default extensions (e.g., `".foo""` or `".bar"`) is not a solution. Here the request feature will be helpful.

The extension here may be similar to the built-in markdown extension. Its preview can also be open without an editor. A big difference is that it only handle one language and so, the "when" clause guarantees the language of the file.

Even for extensions that use a built-in text editor, in some situations it will be helpful if they can know the language of a file before it is opened.

## Workaround using the current API and Its Limitation 

Though `when` clause in package.json correctly reflects this setting, from the extension's code it seems difficult to check it. Extensions can read the glob patterns in `files.associations` setting via `WorkspaceConfiguration`'s `get()` method but anyway VS Code's built-in matcher is unavailable for URI or string. There are several popular their-party matchers but to completely imitate the behavior to VS Code's one is very hard (and maybe impossible). Also it seems there is an (as far as I know) undocumented rules about precedence between items defined in `files.associations`.
- multiple rules that both match: e.g., `*.json` and `*.config.json` (longer one is always preceded?)
- rules defined in global (user) and workspaces
  - Especially when two keys whose case is different, such as `*.foo` in global and `*.FOO` in workspace, the situation becomes difficult. The object returned by `get()` methods contain both key-value pairs but the extension can not judge which one is from which scope. Then one may have to use `inspect()` and then merge it manually.

Practically it will be very hard for extension authors to perfectly imitate VS Code's behavior about the file association within the current API. This is the reason I request the function.
comments: I don't understand what this request is about. Yes, VS Code has a custom glob match implementation but that's not incompatible with popular 3rd libraries and extension can use those. I am also confused how `when` condition fit into this

title: Copilot Chat 0.43.0: Claude agent mode silent — tool permission stream closes before response received (hook_6)
description:
Type: **Bug**

## Summary

When running Copilot Chat in **Claude agent mode**, the chat window is silent during any turn that requires tool calls. The VS Code output panel logs a specific error from the claude-agent-sdk: the tool permission hook stream (`hook_6`) closes before the permission decision arrives, aborting the tool call before any response can be generated. This started approximately 27 March 2026 and has been consistently reproducible since.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open VS Code (1.115) with GitHub Copilot Chat 0.43.0.
2. In Copilot Chat, switch to Claude agent mode (e.g. `claude-sonnet-4.6`).
3. Issue any prompt that requires tool use — file reads, web fetches, shell commands, or git operations.
4. Observe that the chat window shows no activity and no response while the agent is working.
5. Open the VS Code output panel and filter to the Copilot Chat output channel.

## Expected Behaviour

The agent narrates progress and shows tool call activity in the chat window throughout the turn. A final response is delivered when the turn completes.

## Actual Behaviour

The chat window is silent throughout. The output panel logs one or more instances of:

```
[error] claude-agent-sdk stderr: Error in hook callback hook_6: Error: Tool permission stream closed before response received
    at F58.read (file:///home/ciara/.vscode-server/extensions/github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/cli.js:8246:342)
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:103:5)
    at async file:///home/ciara/.vscode-server/extensions/github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/cli.js:16742:1569
```

The claude-agent-sdk receives no tool permission response and aborts the tool call. Because the turn never completes, no output is generated for the chat window.

## Session Log Evidence

The Copilot Chat debug log (exported via "Export All Prompts as JSON") shows the symptom directly.

**ChatMLCancelation — aborted turn (duration 157 ms, no work done):**
```json
{
  "id": "37bfd4cc",
  "kind": "request",
  "type": "ChatMLCancelation",
  "name": "Claude Copilot Proxy",
  "metadata": {
    "requestType": "ChatMessages",
    "model": "claude-sonnet-4.6",
    "startTime": "2026-04-10T11:35:40.129Z",
    "endTime": "2026-04-10T11:35:40.286Z",
    "duration": 157
  }
}
```

**Multiple empty-response turns in the same session** (ids: `4d5a311f`, `a799007f`, `51c1061c`) — turns where Claude responded with an empty message because the tool call it depended on was never completed.

**Contrast:** Turns where no tool call was needed completed normally and their responses were relayed to the chat window without issue. The failure is specific to the tool permission stage, not to text relay.

## Analysis

The tool permission hook system in the claude-agent-sdk uses a stream (`hook_6`) to deliver the allow/deny decision for each tool call. That stream is closing before the decision arrives. Possible causes:

1. **Timing regression** — the permission decision now arrives after a threshold that causes the read stream to time out or be garbage-collected.
2. **`use-responses-api` experiment interaction** — two previously closed related issues (#297982, #297879) both had the `use-responses-api` A/B experiment active. If switching from the streaming completions API to the Responses API changes the timing or lifecycle of hook callbacks, this could cause premature stream closure.
3. **SDK version mismatch** — a version incompatibility between the claude-agent-sdk bundled in `cli.js` and the callback contract expected by the extension host.

The obfuscated symbol `F58.read` at `cli.js:8246:342` is the stream reader for the hook callback; the async dispatch at `cli.js:16742:1569` is the main agent loop awaiting the permission result.

## Onset

The problem began approximately **27 March 2026**, coinciding with the VS Code 1.113 release cycle. VS Code 1.113 introduced bridging of MCP servers to Claude agent sessions and changed session listing to use the official Claude agent SDK API. None of the 1.113–1.115 release notes document a change to tool permission or hook callback handling, suggesting the cause may be a server-side experiment rollout rather than a VS Code binary change.

## Related Issues

- microsoft/vscode#297982 — Copilot Chat incorrectly stating MCP tools unavailable (closed not_planned; had `use-responses-api` experiment active)
- microsoft/vscode#297879 — Unable to access any available MCP (closed not_planned; had `use-responses-api` experiment active)

## Environment

Extension version: **0.43.0**
VS Code version: **1.115** (released 2026-04-07)
OS: **Linux x64** (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
Running in: VS Code Server (remote/Codespace)

<details>
<summary>System Info</summary>

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Shell | bash |
| Remote | VS Code Server on Linux WSL2 |
| Extension host | github.copilot-chat-0.43.0 |
| claude-agent-sdk | bundled in dist/cli.js |

</details>
comments:
## Session Log Attachment

Sanitised Copilot Chat session debug log (exported via "Export All Prompts as JSON") from a session exhibiting this bug.

**Sanitisation applied:**
- `requestMessages` omitted from all entries (contained full system prompt and conversation history)
- File paths redacted to `[HOME]/`
- Context compaction summaries replaced with `[context compaction summary — omitted]`
- Request/server UUIDs preserved (needed for correlation)

**Gist:** https://gist.github.com/minouris/83c0b3a68ef6ef31d93d0bf094e6a9f8

### What the log shows

The session contains 3 prompts, 86 total log entries. Key entries demonstrating the bug:

| Entry ID | Type | Duration | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `37bfd4cc` | `ChatMLCancelation` | 157 ms | Turn immediately aborted — stream closure during tool permission |
| `4d5a311f` | `ChatMLSuccess` | — | Empty response — dependent tool call never completed |
| `a799007f` | `ChatMLSuccess` | — | Empty response — dependent tool call never completed |
| `51c1061c` | `ChatMLSuccess` | — | Empty response — dependent tool call never completed |

For contrast: turns where no tool call was required (e.g. `a64f6731`, `5ae26250`) completed normally and their responses reached the chat window, confirming relay itself works when tool calls do not abort.

The `ChatMLCancelation` at `37bfd4cc` occurred within 157 ms — far too fast to have dispatched or received any tool result. This matches exactly the timing of a permission stream that closes immediately on open.

Thanks for raising this: @minouris 

Confirming this issue — scope is wider than tool-call silencing, and the root cause may be an API routing compatibility break specific to Claude/Anthropic models

We're seeing the same regression on VS Code 1.115.0 (commit 41dd792b) + Copilot Chat 0.43.0, running in WSL2 on Windows with a Copilot Pro+ account.

Visible impact: Claude models produce no response whatsoever in the Copilot Chat panel — the chat window shows no activity, no message, no error regardless of which Claude model is selected. The failure is completely silent to the user.

Log evidence: When Claude Sonnet 4.6 is selected, no [panel/editAgent] log entry appears at all — the substantive request never routes to Claude. Only gpt-4o-mini entries appear for [title] and [progressMessages]. By contrast, selecting GPT-5.2-Codex routes correctly, with gpt-5.2-codex correctly appearing for [panel/editAgent]. This appears to be specific to Claude/Anthropic models — all GPT models tested work correctly end-to-end.

Hypothesis on root cause: Copilot's backend uses separate API paths for Claude vs GPT models — Claude requires routing to Anthropic's native /v1/messages endpoint rather than the OpenAI-compatible /chat/completions path that GPT models use. Third-party proxy projects that wrap the Copilot API explicitly implement this routing fork with a useMessagesApi flag for Claude-family models. If the 0.43.0 agent mode changes (or the VS Code 1.113–1.115 Claude agent SDK bridging changes) broke or regressed that routing fork, Claude requests would fail silently while GPT requests continue working normally — which is exactly what we're observing.

This would also explain why the same pattern has been reported on other OpenAI-compatible frontends (such as Open Web UI) when Claude models are selected — it's not VS Code-specific behaviour, it's a mismatch between how Claude's API responses are structured versus what the agent orchestration layer expects.

Environment:

VS Code: 1.115.0 (41dd792b5e652393e7787322889ed5fdc58bd75b)
Copilot Chat: 0.43.0
OS: WSL2 (Ubuntu), Windows host
Copilot plan: Pro+
Clean single ~/.vscode-server install, no duplicate extensions, no local model overrides in settings.json

title: Hide "Install" Button from MCP Server Gallery when Server provides neither packages nor remotes
description: When the MCP Registry is configured to be used as an allowlist, VSCode allows to only run those MCP Servers that are part of the registry. However, some MCP Servers have custom installation mechanisms and can't be modeled as either `packages` or `remotes`. The MCP Registry Specification acknowledges this: > For MCP servers that follow a custom installation path or are embedded in applications without standalone packages, use the websiteUrl field to direct users to setup documentation: > > ```json > { > "$schema": "https://static.modelcontextprotocol.io/schemas/2025-12-11/server.schema.json", > "name": "io.modelcontextprotocol.anonymous/embedded-mcp", > "description": "MCP server embedded in a Desktop app", > "websiteUrl": "https://anonymous.modelcontextprotocol.io/embedded-mcp-guide", > "version": "0.1.0" > } > ``` > > https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/blob/main/docs/reference/server-json/generic-server-json.md#server-with-custom-installation-path There is various practical examples: - XCode wants to use the command `xcrun mcpbridge`, with the binary being distributed as part of xcode: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/giving-external-agents-access-to-xcode - Playwright CLI wants the user to set up the server via `npx playwright init-agents --loop=vscode`: https://playwright.dev/docs/test-agents - Moderne wants the server to be set up via `mod config agent-tools install`, which generates OS-dependent code: https://docs.moderne.io/user-documentation/agent-tools/mcp/ Still, to make these work with the allowlist, we need to put something in the registry -- and that entry won't have packages or remotes. However, in VSCode's MCP Gallery, the Server is still shown with an Install button. Clicking it will fail every time with an error message. **Instead of failing with an error, VSCode should hide the install button(s) of MCP Server entries without any package or remote.**
comments: If the MCP server does not provide any packages, why is it in the gallery at all? Could you clarify the use case? Closing as this seems like the server should not be listed if it provides nothing to install.

title: Agents app - context convenience features from VS Code
description: In "Agents - Insider" app: - it would be good to be able to have the nice context features from VS Code such as copy a symbol reference from a code file and paste into the prompt. e.g. here is the VSCode example:
comments: @mjbvz can you maybe help out? I can not copy/paste a method into the chat input in the agents app and get the behaviour of seeing the reference added as context. It works fine in VS Code (even for Copilot CLI sessions). This makes me wonder if there is a contribution missing in the agents app that you maybe recently added?

This is powered by a `DocumentPasteEditProvider`: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/ad2f77efc987779a66ff5d1c6372c9edf87182b0/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/input/editor/chatPasteProviders.ts#L571

Our custom paste providers currently are only in-memory for a given window. They don't write back to the system clipboard or even to a clipboard that can be used across VS Code instances

I looked into this when I first added the feature but the dom clipboard APIs at the time didn't seem to support it and I couldn't figure out how to get it working using the Electron APIs either. Now however I think we support it using using a web mime value (`web application/vnd.code.customData`)

Here's an example where we do something similar today:

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/ad2f77efc987779a66ff5d1c6372c9edf87182b0/src/vs/platform/clipboard/browser/clipboardService.ts#L221
@mjbvz thanks, but just to clarify: this is in the same window, between an editor opened as modal (still same window) and pasting to the chat input in the same window.

OK I misunderstood the issue and can confirm this feature also doesn't between a text editor in the agents app and the chat input box

Here's the line that fails:

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/382afbc68c25eca96f3452e5f09a8b9abf650bb8/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/input/editor/chatPasteProviders.ts#L528

Here's an example copied file's uri in the clipboard :  `copilot-pr:/src/extension/prompt/vscode-node/requestLoggerImpl.ts?%7B%22owner%22%3...`

Maybe we should try loading the model in this case instead of only checking the existing models?

@mjbvz ah I see now what the issue is: editors in the modal dispose when the modal closes, so the model cannot be loaded like this anymore. I wonder why do you need to load the modal at all? This will be fixed with Lads work to open editors to the side of chat where you can copy/paste symbols easily: Yes, if you need the model, you should resolve it and then dispose your reference.

title: VS Code: Optional auto-jump to first error line when opening a file
description: Type: Feature Request Summary When opening a file that already contains diagnostics, VS Code shows red markers but does not move the cursor to the first error line. For debugging-heavy workflows, this adds repetitive manual navigation. Request Please add an optional setting to auto-jump to the first diagnostic line when a file is opened. Proposed behavior New setting (default off), for example: editor.autoRevealFirstErrorOnOpen: false When enabled: On file open, if the file has diagnostics, jump to the first error line. Optional scope: current file only include warnings (optional) Keep current default behavior unchanged for users who do not want cursor movement. Why this helps Reduces repetitive F8 / Problems click navigation Speeds up fix loops for static analysis and lint-heavy projects Improves focus in large files with known issues Current workaround Manually press F8 after opening each file Click diagnostics in Problems panel Environment VS Code + language diagnostics (e.g., Intelephense / linters / static analyzers) Reproducible with files that already have diagnostics before opening VS Code version: Code - Insiders 1.115.0-insider (fc23f2d26631c6a2c4bf9f69506ea74c90a32804, 2026-03-31T17:13:42+01:00) OS version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.28000 Modes:

title: Copilot Chat should support cross-device cloud conversation memory for repository workflows
description: Type: Feature Request Summary When using Copilot Chat for multi-day coding tasks, conversation context is lost across devices/sessions unless users manually copy and paste summaries. For repository-based workflows, this causes repeated setup, repeated debugging, and lower productivity. Problem Current behavior requires manual context handoff. Long-running tasks (static analysis tuning, refactor iterations, toolchain setup) are frequently interrupted by session/device changes. Users often work mostly with text/code context, so storage size is small and this should be technically feasible. Expected behavior Persist chat history in cloud for the signed-in user. Allow seamless continuation of context across devices. Optionally bind memory to repository scope. Optionally allow users to store structured “project memory” in GitHub (private by default, user-controlled). Actual behavior New device/session often starts without prior conversational context. Users must manually reconstruct prior decisions and tool settings. Why this matters Reduces repeated troubleshooting. Improves continuity for complex engineering tasks. Better user experience for professional development workflows. Suggested solution Add opt-in cloud memory for Copilot Chat history. Add repository-scoped memory mode with clear privacy controls. Provide retention settings and explicit “forget/reset” controls. Surface a “resume previous context” entry point when opening the same repo. Environment Product: GitHub Copilot Chat Client: VS Code Use case: Multi-session static analysis/tooling setup across machines VS Code version: Code - Insiders 1.115.0-insider (fc23f2d26631c6a2c4bf9f69506ea74c90a32804, 2026-03-31T17:13:42+01:00) OS version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.28000 Modes:
comments:
I really want to +1 this.

I use Copilot Chat across long-running tasks that span multiple days and multiple machines. Right now, losing context between sessions means I have to keep re-explaining prior decisions, tool settings, and what we already tried. That repetition adds a lot of friction.

What would make a big difference:
- Opt-in cloud memory tied to my signed-in account
- Optional repository-scoped memory
- Clear retention controls plus easy forget/reset options
- A simple "resume previous context" prompt when I reopen the same repo

For enterprise users, control and transparency are critical. If this is added, the scope, retention, and reset behavior should be explicit and easy to manage.

title: VSCode MCP Task Compliance - Terrible UX
description:
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: Latest (pre-release)
- VS Code Version: 1.110+
- OS Version: Windows 11 Pro 10.0.22631
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Agent mode (MCP tool calls)
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-4.5 (model-independent, issue is in the MCP client layer)
- Logs: N/A (protocol behavior, not a crash)

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Configure an MCP server (Streamable HTTP transport) that declares `tasks.requests.tools.call` in its server capabilities
2. The server exposes tools with `execution.taskSupport: "optional"` that wrap async processes (e.g. deploying infrastructure, triggering CI pipelines, running test suites)
3. In Agent mode, invoke one of these tools
4. The server returns a `CreateTaskResult` immediately with `taskId`, `status: "working"`, and a `pollInterval`
5. The conversation is completely frozen. No indication a task was created. No status updates. No way to continue chatting. The window is unusable until the task reaches a terminal state.

## The UX problem

When an MCP tool returns a Task (per the [2025-11-25 spec](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/utilities/tasks)), the chat window becomes a dead zone. The user experience is:

- A generic spinner with no context. No mention of a task, no task ID, no status, no indication of what's being waited on.
- The conversation is completely locked. You cannot ask follow-up questions, invoke other tools, or interact with the chat in any way.
- There is no cancel button, no "continue without waiting" option, no way to dismiss the pending result.
- If the task takes 5 minutes, the window is dead for 5 minutes. If the task takes 3 weeks, the window is dead for 3 weeks.
- Closing and reopening the chat loses all task context, even though the task is still alive on the server.

This is pragmatically unusable for any MCP tool that wraps an async process. Cloud deployments, CI/CD pipeline statuses, many SaaS MCP use cases, tracking statuses etc.: these are the exact use cases Tasks was designed for, and none of them work in practice because the client blocks.

The MCP Tasks spec exists specifically to decouple tool invocation from result retrieval. The server does its part: it returns immediately with a task ID and polls correctly. The entire point is that the conversation should continue while the task runs in the background.

## Expected behavior

When a `tools/call` returns a `CreateTaskResult`:

1. Return to the LLM immediately with the task metadata (taskId, status, statusMessage) and any content from the response, so it can inform the user: "I've submitted your order. Task ID: abc123. I'll let you know when it completes."
2. Show a visible task status indicator in the chat (task name, current status, elapsed time)
3. Allow the conversation to continue while the task polls in the background
4. When the task reaches a terminal state, surface the result as a new message or notification
5. Allow the user to cancel a running task from the chat UI
6. Persist task awareness across chat sessions (tasks have TTL and survive reconnection by spec design)

<details>
<summary>Potential cause in the codebase</summary>

VS Code has full MCP Tasks protocol support. The issue is not missing implementation but that the task lifecycle is treated as a transparent transport detail rather than a UX concept.

### `callTool()` awaits the task result Promise

In [`src/vs/workbench/contrib/mcp/common/mcpServerRequestHandler.ts`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/workbench/contrib/mcp/common/mcpServerRequestHandler.ts) (~line 517):

```typescript
async callTool(params, token?, onStatusMessage?): Promise<MCP.CallToolResult> {
    const response = await this.sendRequest<MCP.CallToolRequest, MCP.CallToolResult | MCP.CreateTaskResult>(
        { method: 'tools/call', params }, token
    );
    if (isTaskResult(response)) {
        const task = new McpTask<MCP.CallToolResult>(response.task, token, onStatusMessage);
        this._taskManager.adoptClientTask(task);
        task.setHandler(this);
        return task.result.finally(() => {       // <-- blocks here until terminal state
            this._taskManager.abandonClientTask(task.id);
        });
    }
    return response;
}
```

`task.result` is a Promise that resolves only when the task reaches `completed`, `failed`, or `cancelled`. The LLM's tool call is suspended on this Promise, so the entire conversation turn blocks regardless of how quickly the server returned the task.

### The polling loop runs silently

In the same file (~line 678), `McpTask` polls `tasks/get` using `task.pollInterval` (or a 2-second default). Status changes are tracked internally but never surfaced to the user.

### Immediate response metadata is discarded

MCP servers can include actionable content alongside the task in response metadata (e.g. order confirmations or status summaries the LLM could present immediately). Because `callTool()` blocks on the terminal result, this content never reaches the LLM.

</details>
comments:
Thanks for creating this issue! It looks like you may be using an old version of VS Code; the latest stable release is 1.115.0. Please try upgrading to the latest version and checking whether this issue remains.

Happy Coding!

Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/308549

I have a WIP on https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/tree/connor4312/tasks-backgrounding, will probably finish it in a week or so.

Also tried all the combinations of `isDefault`. Also tried all the permutations. Also tried for `Run` and `Debug` kind, it works for them, only problem with `Coverage`. Maybe I'm doing something wrong but I'm running out of ideas. Thanks in advance
comments: Don't mind that the example is different from the picture. Just wanted to give a clean repro. Probably the best proof: As you see continuous run has coverage options but coverage itself doesn't...

title: Port Forward: doesn't show any error message when underlying ssh raised error
description:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! bug_report_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- 🕮 Read our guide about submitting issues: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Submitting-Bugs-and-Suggestions -->
<!-- 🔎 Search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->
<!-- 🧪 Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->
<!-- 💡 Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->
<!-- 🔧 Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes/No

<!-- 🪓 If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- 📣 Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->
- VS Code Version: 1.114.0
- OS Version: Windows 11

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Connected to a remote SSH linux server and started an api listening to port 9090. VS Code showed auto-forwarded port, but couldn't open it in browser.
2. Port forwarding in terminal `ssh 9090:localhost:9090 user@server` showed ssh had blocked this with error `bind: permission error`. But VS Code did not show this SSH error message at all, and port seemed to be forwarded when it actually wasn't.

Here VS Code should check & show if port forwarding was actually done or if any SSH error occurred.

title: Unable to use Copilot/Local in multiple Chat Editors
description:
I'm not sure when this broke, perhaps its been like this from day 1,
But I know I've used Copilot CLI in multiple editors in the past

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7ef02e29-59fb-4d1f-9e6c-23d651df5b46)
comments:
Debugging, looks like `NewChatSessionInPlaceAction` doesn't actually specify which editor to target. It just targets the active editor

Easy fix is probably just to focus the chat editor when you click on these widgets

title: Exclude .jj (Jujutsu VCS) directory by default
description:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

VS code should have an entry for `.jj` in `files.exclude` by default. The directory contains [Jujutsu](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/) VCS files, just like `.git` and `.hg`.

title: ask_questions auto-replies with incorrect answers in autopilot mode
description: I saw the agent going down a weird rabbit hole, and it started with this question. I didn't type that answer, and the answer is totally wrong. I have `chat.autoReply` disabled but am in autopilot mode I thought we said that this feature was only for evals, and wouldn't be used in the product? Why would we feed in random incorrect answers, which the model then thinks came from me?
comments: I believe we decided that the model should indicate the user is not available to answer and to use best judgement in these cases.

I see here we're just selecting the default 

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/fc5edfe78eb2cf345ae03b85cb95193d8ac8b52e/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/tools/builtinTools/askQuestionsTool.ts#L490-L506

Thanks for pointing that out.

On Fri, Apr 10, 2026, 10:18 AM Megan Rogge ***@***.***> wrote:

> *meganrogge* left a comment (microsoft/vscode#308934)
> <https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/308934#issuecomment-4224782392>
>
> I see here we're just selecting the default
>
>
> https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/fc5edfe78eb2cf345ae03b85cb95193d8ac8b52e/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/tools/builtinTools/askQuestionsTool.ts#L490-L506
>
> —
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/308934#issuecomment-4224782392>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/B7VN24LZSDDHSPIPYMW4LI34VEGEDAVCNFSM6AAAAACXTJ2JCSVHI2DSMVQWIX3LMV43OSLTON2WKQ3PNVWWK3TUHM2DEMRUG44DEMZZGI>
> .
> You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.Message
> ID: ***@***.***>
>
Ohhh, I didn't realize it was a multi-choice and not a free response. Sorry, should have grabbed the log. That's better than 4o-mini generating text as I assumed but it's still wrong IMO
This was an important decision point in the conversation and led the agent far down the totally wrong path

title: Browser: Decide on a global keyboard shortcut for opening browser (and for showing next to View->Browser)
description: We don't yet have a global keyboard shortcut for Browser. We only have shortcuts that work conditionally when the browser is focused. So we need to brainstorm what would be a good shortcut to use for this action. Currently, Browser is the odd one out in the View menu because it doesn't show a keyboard shortcut: * Related, earlier PR: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/308632
comments: Possibly `Ctrl+Alt+/`? It sort of reflects the Terminal's `` Ctrl+` `` shortcut and `/` gives URL to me

You don't think it would have an association/confusion with comment syntax? These are the current slash-containing commands on Mac: And the ones on Windows:

To verify:
* Make sure that the View->Browser menu item shows a shortcut next to it: `Ctrl+Alt+/` (`⌘⌥/` on macOS)
* Also validate that the shortcut actually works

title: Incorrect command line argument quoting/escaping in `extHostTerminalShellIntegration.ts`
description:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! bug_report_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- 🕮 Read our guide about submitting issues: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Submitting-Bugs-and-Suggestions -->
<!-- 🔎 Search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->
<!-- 🧪 Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->
<!-- 💡 Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->
<!-- 🔧 Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

Version: 1.116.0-insider (system setup)
Commit: 1ec46de152cab65d0304db62e2aa0fcb6e1a5b1f
Date: 2026-04-09T09:55:17-07:00
Electron: 39.8.5
ElectronBuildId: 13703022
Chromium: 142.0.7444.265
Node.js: 22.22.1
V8: 14.2.231.22-electron.0
OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

This is regarding the follow suspicious code, which does not appear to properly handle converting an executable/command path and an arguments array (which should always be assumed to not contain quoting/escaping, as that is a 'shell command line' concept), to a shell command line:

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/c5729b10dcc60970b844987fc751a831d0e7ae3b/src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHostTerminalShellIntegration.ts#L207-L223

Some related background:
[Everyone quotes command line arguments the wrong way](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/archive/blogs/twistylittlepassagesallalike/everyone-quotes-command-line-arguments-the-wrong-way)
[How Command Line Parameters Are Parsed](https://daviddeley.com/autohotkey/parameters/parameters.htm)
[CommandLineToArgvW function (shellapi.h)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/shellapi/nf-shellapi-commandlinetoargvw)

AI generated bug details:

# Bug: `TerminalShellIntegration.executeCommand(executable, args)` serializes structured input into shell text using naive quoting

## Summary

The `TerminalShellIntegration.executeCommand(executable, args)` overload looks like it accepts structured `argv`-style input, but the current implementation in `src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHostTerminalShellIntegration.ts` simply builds a single shell command line string:

```ts
let commandLineValue = commandLineOrExecutable;
if (args) {
	for (const arg of args) {
		const wrapInQuotes = !arg.match(/["'`]/) && arg.match(/\s/);
		if (wrapInQuotes) {
			commandLineValue += ` "${arg}"`;
		} else {
			commandLineValue += ` ${arg}`;
		}
	}
}
```

This means the executable token is copied verbatim and the arguments are appended with only a minimal quoting heuristic before the whole string is reparsed by the active shell.

## Problem

This serialization is shell-agnostic and incomplete:

- the **command/executable portion is not quoted or escaped at all**,
- arguments are only quoted in a narrow whitespace-only case,
- literal quote characters inside arguments are not escaped,
- shell metacharacters like `;`, `&`, `|`, `<`, `>`, `` ` ``, and `$()` are not handled safely,
- and quoting/escaping rules differ significantly across shells and platforms (`zsh`, `bash`, `pwsh`, `cmd`, etc.).

As a result, this API overload does not preserve structured input reliably. Both the executable token and the argument boundaries can change when the shell reparses the constructed command line.

## Why this matters

An API shaped like:

```ts
executeCommand(executable: string, args: string[])
```

strongly suggests that `executable` and each item in `args` are treated as literal tokens. But the current behavior is really “best-effort shell string construction”, which is a weaker and more error-prone contract.

At minimum, arguments containing literal double quotes are mishandled. More broadly, this can lead to incorrect `argv` at the target process or unexpected shell interpretation.

## Relevant code path

1. `InternalTerminalShellIntegration.executeCommand(...)` builds `commandLineValue`
2. `MainThreadTerminalShellIntegration.$executeCommand(...)` forwards the string
3. `TerminalInstance.runCommand(...)` / `sendText(...)` writes it into the PTY
4. the active shell reparses that text and launches the subprocess

So this API does not directly spawn a process from an argument array; it sends a command line string to the shell.

## Example problematic inputs

These should be treated as literal tokens, but may be misparsed by the shell:

- executable: `'/tmp/My Tool'`
- args: `['a"b']`
- args: `['a b"c']`
- args: `['foo;bar']`
- args: `['$(uname)']`

## Expected behavior

Either:

1. the `executeCommand(executable, args)` overload should use shell/platform-specific escaping that preserves the executable token and argument boundaries as accurately as possible for the active shell,

or

2. the API/docs should explicitly state that this overload is only a best-effort conversion to shell text and cannot guarantee exact `argv` preservation.

At minimum, literal quotes inside arguments should be escaped correctly, and the executable token should not be emitted verbatim when it requires quoting.
comments:
(AI generated as well: )

Follow-up note: while looking into this, I noticed a few other places in the VS Code codebase that appear to construct shell command lines from structured executable/arg input using ad hoc quoting/escaping logic. They may be unrelated, but they seem worth a quick review for the same class of issue:

- `src/vs/platform/externalTerminal/node/externalTerminalService.ts`
  - has hand-built command strings for external terminals, including simple `args.join(...)` and a `quote(args)` helper that appears to only wrap whitespace-containing args in double quotes.

- `src/vs/workbench/api/node/extHostMcpNode.ts`
  - `formatSubprocessArguments()` switches to `shell: true` for `.bat`/`.cmd` and appears to use a space-only quoting heuristic.

- `extensions/copilot/src/extension/chatSessions/vscode-node/copilotCLITerminalIntegration.ts`
  - `buildCommandForTerminal()` joins CLI args into shell text, and `quoteArgsForShell()` looks potentially incomplete for shell-specific escaping.

- `src/vs/workbench/contrib/debug/node/debugAdapter.ts`
  - the Windows `.bat`/`.cmd` path appears to manually quote/escape args before spawning through the shell.

To be clear, I have not fully validated each of these as a bug, but they look similar enough architecturally (structured input being flattened back into shell text) that they may be worth checking while this issue is being investigated.

# Two related issues: VS Code 1.114 phantom install running alongside 1.115 + uncontrolled window spawning

**VS Code version:** 1.115.0 (system install)
**OS version:** Windows 11 (x64)
**Investigation date:** 14 April 2026

---

## Issue 1: VS Code 1.114.0 continues running alongside 1.115.0 after upgrade

### Steps to Reproduce (Issue 1)

1. Install VS Code system-wide using the official installer (target path `C:\Program Files\Code` — non-standard)
2. Later install VS Code 1.115.0 via the standard installer (target path `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code`)
3. Observe both versions running simultaneously

### Expected Behaviour (Issue 1)

Installing VS Code 1.115.0 should detect and supersede the existing 1.114.0 installation. Only one stable `Code.exe` should be active.

### Actual Behaviour (Issue 1)

Both installations coexist at separate paths and both launch concurrently:

| Install               | Version       | Path                                 | Registry Uninstall Entry |
| --------------------- | ------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------ |
| Standard installer    | 1.115.0       | `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code` | Present                  |
| Older installer build | **1.114.0**   | `C:\Program Files\Code`              | **Absent**               |

The 1.114.0 installation at `C:\Program Files\Code` has **no Windows registry uninstall entry** (`HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall`). The 1.115.0 installer therefore never detected it and installed to a fresh path rather than upgrading in place.

### Root Cause Analysis (Issue 1)

The 1.114.0 installer placed VS Code at `C:\Program Files\Code` — a shorter path without the `Microsoft VS Code` branding used by the current installer. This path mismatch breaks installer detection/upgrade logic. The NSIS uninstaller (`unins000.exe`) is present inside the folder, but the registry key allowing the 1.115.0 installer to detect "VS Code is already installed here" does not exist.

#### PATH contention

The system `PATH` at time of investigation contained (relevant entries only, in order):

```text
C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code          <- root exe — 1.115
C:\Program Files\Code\bin                   <- 1.114 bin shim  <- "code" resolves here first
C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code Insiders\bin
C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\bin      <- 1.115 bin shim
```

`C:\Program Files\Code\bin` appears **before** `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\bin`. Any terminal or tool invoking `code` receives the **1.114.0** shim. File associations and Start Menu shortcuts that reference `Code.exe` directly continue to open **1.115.0**. The same user session runs different versions depending on the launch method.

#### Shared user data (critical)

Both installations declare identical `product.json` values:

| `product.json` field | 1.114.0 value                    | 1.115.0 value                    |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| `dataFolderName`     | `.vscode` => `%APPDATA%\Code`    | `.vscode` => `%APPDATA%\Code`    |
| `win32MutexName`     | `vscode`                         | `vscode`                         |
| `win32RegValueName`  | `VSCode`                         | `VSCode`                         |
| `applicationName`    | `code`                           | `code`                           |
| `quality`            | `stable`                         | `stable`                         |
| `target`             | `system`                         | `system`                         |

Both installations read and write the **same** `%APPDATA%\Roaming\Code\User\` directory — settings, extensions, workspace storage, extension global storage, and Copilot Chat history are all shared. Running both simultaneously creates a shared-state race condition.

---

## Issue 2: VS Code 1.115.0 creates uncontrolled new windows automatically

### Steps to Reproduce (Issue 2)

1. Have VS Code installed as described above (1.114 phantom + 1.115 standard)
2. Start Windows session or open a new VS Code window
3. Observe multiple new VS Code windows appearing without user action

### Expected Behaviour (Issue 2)

VS Code restores only the windows that were open in the previous session, once, without spawning additional unsolicited windows.

### Actual Behaviour (Issue 2)

At time of investigation, **28+ `Code.exe` processes** were active from both installations simultaneously:

```text
~14 processes from  C:\Program Files\Code                   (1.114.0)
~14 processes from  C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code      (1.115.0)
```

New VS Code windows continued appearing after the session was established.

### Root Cause Analysis (Issue 2)

Multiple compounding factors identified:

#### Factor 1 — Shared `windowsState` triggers dual restoration

`%APPDATA%\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\storage.json` stores `windowsState` (including `openedWindows[]`). VS Code default `window.restoreWindows = "all"`. Both installations read the **same** `windowsState` on startup and each independently restores the full previous session — every workspace opens twice.

The following setting is active in global `settings.json` (no profile-level override):

```json
"window.newWindowProfile": "Markdown-Editor"
```

In 1.115.0, profile-scoped window management was revised; the interaction between `window.newWindowProfile` and shared `windowsState` across two concurrent instances may be relevant.

#### Factor 2 — WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode Store app (high suspicion)

A third-party Windows Store app is running persistently:

- **Package:** `15722UsefulApp.WorkspaceLauncherforVSCode` v1.26.0.0
- **Publisher:** UsefulApps
- **Binary:** `C:\Program Files\WindowsApps\15722UsefulApp.WorkspaceLauncherforVSCode_1.26.0.0_x64__s4b834ss2syh6\WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode.exe`
- **Status:** Running at time of investigation

This app brokers workspace-open requests to VS Code. With two installations sharing the same `code` command namespace and the same mutex, it may be sending open-workspace signals that are received and acted on by both instances simultaneously.

#### Factor 3 — Shared `win32MutexName = vscode`

The `vscode` mutex normally prevents duplicate instances from opening the same workspace. Because both 1.114.0 and 1.115.0 declare the **same** mutex name, the OS sees them as the same application for single-instance purposes — but they are separate processes at different executable paths. Mutex contention behaviour in this edge case needs investigation.

#### Factor 4 — `window.restoreWindows` acting on stale shared state

Each of the 28+ concurrent processes contributes `openedWindows` entries that the other instance then attempts to restore, creating a feedback loop.

---

## Environment

```text
OS:             Windows 11 x64
Code 1.115.0:   C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code          (system installer, registered)
Code 1.114.0:   C:\Program Files\Code                       (system installer, NOT registered)
Code Insiders:  C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code Insiders (1.116.0-insider, registered)
User data dir:  C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\  (shared by both stable installs)
Workspace storage entries: 60
```

Also running (potentially relevant):

- `WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode` v1.26.0.0 — 15722UsefulApp (Microsoft Store)
- `CmdPalVsCode` v1.6.0.0 — JonahFintzDEV (Microsoft Store)

---

## Suggested Investigation Areas

1. **Installer detection breadth** — Should the 1.115.0 system installer scan all `C:\Program Files\*Code*` variants for existing stable installations, not only the canonical `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code` path?

2. **Registry key guarantee** — Should the installer write (or verify) an uninstall registry key even on a fresh install, to ensure future installers can detect and upgrade it?

3. **Mutex collision between same-quality installs** — When two `stable` system installations share `win32MutexName`, `win32RegValueName`, and `applicationName`, what is the expected single-instance behaviour? Is there a guard preventing both instances from acting on the same `windowsState`?

4. **`windowsState` claim/lock mechanism** — Should VS Code add a session-lock to `windowsState` so only the first instance to start claims and restores it, rather than all running instances independently restoring the same state?

5. **`window.newWindowProfile` regression in 1.115.0** — The uncontrolled window spawning was reported as coinciding specifically with the 1.115.0 upgrade. Is there a known regression in profile-aware window restoration or `window.newWindowProfile` handling in 1.115?

---

## Note on User Data Safety

Copilot Chat history and user settings are stored in:

```text
%APPDATA%\Roaming\Code\User\workspaceStorage\
%APPDATA%\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\github.copilot-chat\
```

This directory is **not** inside either installation folder. Removing `C:\Program Files\Code` (the 1.114 phantom install) will not affect chat history, settings, or extensions.

# Remediation Plan — VS Code Phantom Install & Window Spawning

> Was mostly followed and was 
> Question is : Why was C:\Programe\Code for v.1114 created that caused the issues?

**Companion to:** [Issue Report](./vscode-github-issue-1114-phantom-install-and-window-popups.md) | [Investigation Methodology](./vscode-github-issue-1114-investigation-methodology.md)
**Date:** 14 April 2026
**Platform:** Windows 11 x64, PowerShell 7 (run as Administrator where noted)

> **Safety guarantee:** Every step in this plan is reversible or verified before execution.
> Copilot Chat history and all user settings live in `%APPDATA%\Roaming\Code\User\` — entirely
> separate from any installation folder. Nothing in this plan touches that directory.

---

## Pre-Remediation Checklist

Before starting, verify the following:

```powershell
# 1. Confirm user data location is safe
Test-Path "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\github.copilot-chat"
Test-Path "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\workspaceStorage"

# 2. Record current workspace storage entry count (baseline)
(Get-ChildItem "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\workspaceStorage" -Directory).Count

# 3. Confirm the two stable installs and their versions
Get-Content "C:\Program Files\Code\e7fb5e96c0\resources\app\package.json" |
  ConvertFrom-Json | Select-Object name, version    # expected: 1.114.0

Get-Content "C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\41dd792b5e\resources\app\package.json" |
  ConvertFrom-Json | Select-Object name, version    # expected: 1.115.0
```

All three `Test-Path` checks must return `True` before proceeding. The version checks confirm the two installs are still exactly as investigated.

---

## Phase 1 — Stop All Running VS Code Instances

> **Why first:** Removing an installation folder or modifying PATH while VS Code is writing to shared
> `windowsState` risks corrupting `storage.json`. Stopping all instances cleanly resets the state.

### Step 1.1 — Save open workspaces (manual)

Before killing processes, **manually save** any open files and note which workspace folders are open. VS Code will restore them from `windowsState` after remediation, but a manual note is a safe fallback.

### Step 1.2 — Terminate all Code processes

```powershell
# Preview what will be stopped
Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.Name -like "Code" } |
  Select-Object Name, Id, Path | Format-Table -AutoSize

# Stop all Code.exe processes
Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.Name -like "Code" } | Stop-Process -Force
```

### Step 1.3 — Verify no Code processes remain

```powershell
$remaining = Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.Name -like "Code" }
if ($remaining) {
    Write-Warning "Remaining Code processes: $($remaining.Count)"
    $remaining | Select-Object Name, Id, Path
} else {
    Write-Host "All Code processes stopped." -ForegroundColor Green
}
```

Expected: no output (zero Code processes). Do not proceed to Phase 2 until this is clean.

---

## Phase 2 — Disable WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode

> **Why:** This Windows Store app is a persistent process that sends workspace-open signals to VS Code.
> While two conflicting installs exist on the system it is a live trigger for duplicate windows.
> Disabling it now prevents it from re-spawning windows during the subsequent steps.

### Step 2.1 — Suspend the app (non-destructive, reversible)

```powershell
# Disable the app for the current user — does NOT uninstall it
Get-AppxPackage "15722UsefulApp.WorkspaceLauncherforVSCode" |
  Disable-AppxPackage -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
```

> If you rely on this app for workspace quick-launch, **skip Step 2.1** and instead re-enable it
> **after** Phase 4 is complete and only one `code` command exists in PATH.

### Step 2.2 — Verify it is no longer running

```powershell
Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.Name -like "*WorkspaceLauncher*" }
```

Expected: no output.

---

## Phase 3 — Remove the Phantom 1.114.0 Installation

> **Why:** The phantom install at `C:\Program Files\Code` is the root cause of both issues.
> It has its own NSIS uninstaller. Using it directly is safe and complete.
>
> **User data is unaffected.** This folder contains only program binaries.
> `%APPDATA%\Roaming\Code\User\` is not touched.

### Step 3.1 — Confirm uninstaller exists

```powershell
Test-Path "C:\Program Files\Code\unins000.exe"
```

Expected: `True`. If `False`, skip to Step 3.2 (manual folder removal).

### Step 3.2 — Run the NSIS uninstaller (recommended)

Open an **elevated PowerShell** (`Run as Administrator`):

```powershell
Start-Process "C:\Program Files\Code\unins000.exe" -Wait
```

A GUI uninstaller dialog will appear. Accept the defaults and let it complete. It will remove all files under `C:\Program Files\Code\` and any shortcuts it created.

> **Alternative (if uninstaller fails or hangs):** Manual removal — see Step 3.3.

### Step 3.3 — Manual removal fallback (only if 3.2 fails)

```powershell
# Requires elevated PowerShell
Remove-Item "C:\Program Files\Code" -Recurse -Force
```

> This is safe. The folder contains only binaries. No user data is stored here.

### Step 3.4 — Verify the phantom is gone

```powershell
Test-Path "C:\Program Files\Code"
```

Expected: `False`. If `True`, Step 3.2 or 3.3 did not complete — do not proceed.

---

## Phase 4 — Remediate PATH

> **Why:** Even after removing `C:\Program Files\Code`, its `bin` entry may remain in the system
> `PATH` environment variable (the uninstaller does not always clean PATH). A stale PATH entry
> pointing to a non-existent directory is harmless but should be removed for cleanliness.
> More urgently: if any remnant entries remain, `code` will fail to resolve.

### Step 4.1 — Inspect current Path entries

```powershell
# Check Machine PATH
[System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("PATH", "Machine") -split ";" |
  Where-Object { $_ -like "*\Code*" -or $_ -like "*vscode*" } |
  ForEach-Object { "[$_]" }

# Check User PATH
[System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("PATH", "User") -split ";" |
  Where-Object { $_ -like "*\Code*" -or $_ -like "*vscode*" } |
  ForEach-Object { "[$_]" }
```

Look for any entry referencing `C:\Program Files\Code\bin` or `C:\Program Files\Code`.

### Step 4.2 — Remove stale PATH entry (Machine scope, requires elevation)

```powershell
# Run as Administrator
$machinePath = [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("PATH", "Machine")
$cleanedPath = ($machinePath -split ";" |
  Where-Object { $_ -notmatch [regex]::Escape("C:\Program Files\Code") }) -join ";"
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("PATH", $cleanedPath, "Machine")
Write-Host "Machine PATH updated." -ForegroundColor Green
```

### Step 4.3 — Remove stale PATH entry (User scope, if present)

```powershell
$userPath = [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("PATH", "User")
$cleanedUserPath = ($userPath -split ";" |
  Where-Object { $_ -notmatch [regex]::Escape("C:\Program Files\Code") }) -join ";"
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("PATH", $cleanedUserPath, "User")
Write-Host "User PATH updated." -ForegroundColor Green
```

### Step 4.4 — Verify `code` command resolves to 1.115.0 (new terminal session)

Open a **new** PowerShell window (so it picks up the updated PATH) and run:

```powershell
Get-Command code | Select-Object Source, Version
```

Expected:

```text
Source                                       Version
------                                       -------
C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\bin\code  1.115.0.0
```

If it still shows `C:\Program Files\Code\...` the old PATH value is cached in the current session — the new terminal window confirms the permanent state.

---

## Phase 5 — Address Window Restore Behaviour

> **Why:** With the phantom install gone, the dual-restoration loop is broken. However,
> `window.restoreWindows = "all"` (the VS Code default) is still active. If `windowsState`
> accumulated a large `openedWindows` array during the conflict period, the first 1.115.0
> startup may attempt to restore an unusually large number of windows.

### Step 5.1 — Inspect current windowsState

```powershell
$stor = Get-Content "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\storage.json" |
  ConvertFrom-Json
$ws = $stor.windowsState
"Queued windows to restore: $(($ws.openedWindows | Measure-Object).Count)"
```

- **Count 0–5:** Normal. Proceed to Step 5.3 (launch VS Code normally).
- **Count 6+:** Consider Step 5.2 (clear stale window state).

### Step 5.2 — (Optional) Clear stale window state

> Only perform this step if Step 5.1 shows an unexpectedly large `openedWindows` count
> (typically more than the number of workspaces you actually want restored) AND the
> auto-popping windows symptom persists after Phase 3 and 4 are complete.

```powershell
# Backup first
Copy-Item "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\storage.json" `
          "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\storage.json.bak"

# Load and clear only the openedWindows array, preserving all other state
$storPath = "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\storage.json"
$stor = Get-Content $storPath | ConvertFrom-Json
$stor.windowsState.openedWindows = @()
$stor | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 20 | Set-Content $storPath
Write-Host "openedWindows cleared. Backup at storage.json.bak" -ForegroundColor Green
```

### Step 5.3 — (Optional) Set explicit window restore setting

If the auto-popping behaviour recurs after remediation, add this to VS Code settings
(`File > Preferences > Settings` or edit `settings.json` directly):

```json
"window.restoreWindows": "folders"
```

This restores only folder/workspace windows, not individual file windows, reducing noise.
The current `window.newWindowProfile: "Markdown-Editor"` setting is unaffected.

---

## Phase 6 — Re-enable WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode (Optional)

> Only perform this phase if you use the WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode app and disabled it in Phase 2.
> With only one stable `code` install now on the system, the app will resolve correctly.

### Step 6.1 — Re-enable the app

```powershell
Get-AppxPackage "15722UsefulApp.WorkspaceLauncherforVSCode" |
  Enable-AppxPackage -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
```

### Step 6.2 — Test workspace launch

Open the WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode app and launch one workspace. Confirm:

- Only one VS Code window opens
- It opens from `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\Code.exe` (1.115.0)

---

## Phase 7 — Post-Remediation Verification

Run the full verification suite after completing all phases above.

```powershell
Write-Host "`n=== POST-REMEDIATION VERIFICATION ===" -ForegroundColor Cyan

# 1. Phantom install gone
$phantom = Test-Path "C:\Program Files\Code"
Write-Host "Phantom install removed: $(-not $phantom)" -ForegroundColor ($phantom ? "Red" : "Green")

# 2. Only one stable executable
$stableExe = Test-Path "C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\Code.exe"
Write-Host "1.115 stable present:    $stableExe" -ForegroundColor ($stableExe ? "Green" : "Red")

# 3. code command resolves to 1.115
$codeCmd = Get-Command code -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
$codeOk = $codeCmd.Source -like "*Microsoft VS Code*"
Write-Host "code -> Microsoft VS Code: $codeOk  ($($codeCmd.Source))" `
  -ForegroundColor ($codeOk ? "Green" : "Red")

# 4. No stale PATH entries
$staleInPath = $env:PATH -split ";" | Where-Object { $_ -like "*\Program Files\Code*" }
Write-Host "Stale PATH entries: $(if ($staleInPath) { $staleInPath } else { 'None' })" `
  -ForegroundColor ($staleInPath ? "Red" : "Green")

# 5. User data intact
$chatHistory = Test-Path "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\globalStorage\github.copilot-chat"
$wsStorage   = (Get-ChildItem "C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code\User\workspaceStorage" -Directory).Count
Write-Host "Copilot Chat history:    $chatHistory" -ForegroundColor ($chatHistory ? "Green" : "Red")
Write-Host "Workspace storage count: $wsStorage entries" -ForegroundColor Green

# 6. No duplicate Code processes at rest
Start-Sleep -Seconds 2
$codeProcs = Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.Name -eq "Code" }
Write-Host "Running Code processes (at rest): $($codeProcs.Count)" `
  -ForegroundColor ($codeProcs.Count -le 2 ? "Green" : "Yellow")
```

**All-green expected output:**

```text
=== POST-REMEDIATION VERIFICATION ===
Phantom install removed:    True
1.115 stable present:       True
code -> Microsoft VS Code:  True  (C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\bin\code)
Stale PATH entries:         None
Copilot Chat history:       True
Workspace storage count:    60 entries
Running Code processes (at rest): 0
```

---

## Rollback Reference

| Step | Rollback Action |
| ---- | --------------- |
| 2.1 — Disabled WorkspaceLauncher | Re-run Phase 6 to re-enable |
| 3.2 — Ran NSIS uninstaller | Not reversible; re-download 1.114.0 from [https://update.code.visualstudio.com/1.114.0/win32-x64/stable](https://update.code.visualstudio.com/1.114.0/win32-x64/stable) if needed |
| 4.2/4.3 — Removed PATH entries | Machine PATH: `$env:PATH` will still work in existing terminal sessions; add it back via System Properties if needed |
| 5.2 — Cleared windowsState | Restore from `storage.json.bak` created in the same step |

---

## Remediation Phases at a Glance

| Phase | Action | Scope | Elevation Required |
| ----- | ------ | ----- | ------------------ |
| 0 | Pre-check — verify user data is safe | Read-only | No |
| 1 | Stop all running Code processes | Running processes | No |
| 2 | Disable WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode | Store app | No |
| 3 | Run NSIS uninstaller for 1.114.0 | `C:\Program Files\Code` | **Yes** |
| 4 | Remove stale PATH entries | System/User environment | **Yes (Machine PATH)** |
| 5 | Clear stale windowsState (if needed) | `%APPDATA%\Code\User\` | No |
| 6 | Re-enable WorkspaceLauncherForVSCode | Store app | No |
| 7 | Post-remediation verification | Read-only | No |

description: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: No VS Code Version: Insiders Version: 1.116.0-insider (Universal) Commit: 0271b4d2fe910341fa33a2a4748835168c2a7f92 Date: 2026-04-09T10:45:20+02:00 Electron: 39.8.5 ElectronBuildId: 13703022 Chromium: 142.0.7444.265 Node.js: 22.22.1 V8: 14.2.231.22-electron.0 OS: Darwin arm64 24.6.0 OS Version: macOS (fill exact version, for example 14.x or 15.x) Steps to Reproduce: In Copilot Chat, run a tool that returns a chat response resource URI (scheme starts with vscode-chat-response-resource), for example `List Copilot Spaces` or `Get Copilot Space` from the remote MCP server https://github.com/github/github-mcp-server/blob/main/docs/remote-server.md#additional-remote-server-toolsets Observe that the chat UI can render and open the returned resource content. In the same chat session, have the agent/tooling attempt to read that same URI via read_file. Observe failure such as outside workspace and not open in editor, or resource not found for returned URI. Expected: If the chat UI can render a returned chat resource, agent-side read access should also be able to resolve it in-session, or there should be a supported bridge to fetch the content reliably. Actual: The resource is visible in chat/editor UI, but agent-side read_file often cannot access it due to scope checks and/or ephemeral resource lifetime, causing inconsistent behavior. Notes: > [!NOTE] > I'm just regurgitating what the model told me here This looks like a tool-layer access mismatch rather than missing backend data. The issue appears intermittent when resource URIs expire quickly between calls.
comments: I have a draft PR for this https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-copilot-chat/pull/5056, I'll try to test it locally

title: Regression: "e is not iterable" at POe.setItems causes repeated extension host crashes (copilot-chat 0.43.0)
description:
## Summary

`TypeError: e is not iterable` at `POe.setItems` in `github.copilot-chat-0.43.0` crashes the extension host repeatedly. This appears to be a regression of [microsoft/vscode-copilot-release#7993](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-copilot-release/issues/7993) (same call site: `setItems` → `_computeFn` → `_recompute` → `recomputeInitiallyAndOnChange`).

## Environment

- **Copilot Chat Extension Version:** 0.43.0 (stable)
- **VS Code Version:** 1.100+ (stable channel)
- **OS:** Windows 11

## Stack Trace

```
TypeError: e is not iterable
    at POe.setItems (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:19962)
    at Yd._computeFn (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:19701)
    at Yd._recompute (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:11920)
    at Yd.get (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:11254)
    at Yd.reportChanges (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:497)
    at DOe.endUpdate (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:19460)
    at abn (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:19178)
    at Yd.recomputeInitiallyAndOnChange (github.copilot-chat-0.43.0/dist/extension.js:1162:1092)
```

Compare with [vscode-copilot-release#7993](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-copilot-release/issues/7993) (0.27.2025041701):
```
at EX.setItems (...extension.js:419:27542)
at Ba._computeFn (...extension.js:419:27274)
at Ba._recompute (...extension.js:419:21114)
```

Same code path, different minified symbols.

## Impact

- **21 extension host restarts** in a single VS Code session (one day of work)
- Each restart kills all extensions, not just Copilot Chat — Python language server, ESLint, GitLens, etc. all restart
- Extension host process consuming ~4 GB memory before crashes

## Reproduction

Occurs during normal Copilot Chat agent-mode usage (multi-turn conversations with tool calls). Not tied to a specific action — happens intermittently during chat sessions.

## Bisect

- **0.42.3:** No crashes. Stable over multiple days of heavy usage.
- **0.43.0:** Crashes repeatedly (installed 2026-04-08, crashes started immediately).

Rolling back to 0.42.3 eliminates the issue.

## Secondary Errors (likely consequence, not cause)

After the primary crash, the GitHub PR extension sometimes hits API timeouts during recovery, producing a secondary `TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON` when trying to serialize a TLSSocket error object. This appears to be the PR extension failing to handle its own error gracefully during extension host restart, not the root cause.

## Notes

In [vscode-copilot-release#7993](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-copilot-release/issues/7993), @hediet identified the probable cause: `setItems` receives an `undefined` argument. The original reporter updated their extension and it resolved, so no root-cause fix was confirmed. This regression suggests the underlying issue was masked rather than fixed.

title: [Unhandled Error] Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'clearTodoListWidget')
description:
## Error Message
````
Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'clearTodoListWidget')
````

## Stack Trace
    at cC.setModel ([./src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/chatWidget.ts:1984:0](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/41dd792b5e652393e7787322889ed5fdc58bd75b/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/chatWidget.ts#L1984))  
    at Bx.showModel ([./src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widgetHosts/viewPane/chatViewPane.ts:749:0](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/41dd792b5e652393e7787322889ed5fdc58bd75b/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widgetHosts/viewPane/chatViewPane.ts#L749))  
    at async Bx.clear ([./src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widgetHosts/viewPane/chatViewPane.ts:801:0](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/41dd792b5e652393e7787322889ed5fdc58bd75b/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widgetHosts/viewPane/chatViewPane.ts#L801))

<details>
<summary>Raw Stack Trace (minified)</summary>

````
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'clearTodoListWidget')     at cC.setModel (out/vs/sessions/sessions.desktop.main.js:2372:65120)     at Bx.showModel (out/vs/sessions/sessions.desktop.main.js:3093:75430)     at async Bx.clear (out/vs/sessions/sessions.desktop.main.js:3093:76120)
````

</details>

## Details
| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Bucket ID | [`5cf9162c-66b9-8a2a-c2a5-1895e8cedbe0`](https://errors.code.visualstudio.com/bucket/5cf9162c-66b9-8a2a-c2a5-1895e8cedbe0?product=Agents&commit=41dd792b5e652393e7787322889ed5fdc58bd75b) |
| Version | 1.115.0-insider |
| Commit | [`41dd792b`](https://insiders.vscode.dev/github/microsoft/vscode/tree/41dd792b5e652393e7787322889ed5fdc58bd75b) |
| Last Seen | Wed Apr 08 2026 23:17:30 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time) |
| Platforms | Mac, Windows, Linux |
| Product | Agents |

---
*This issue was automatically created from the VS Code Errors Dashboard*
comments:
## �� Error Investigation

### Summary

The error `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'clearTodoListWidget')` occurs when `ChatWidget.setModel()` is called after the widget has been disposed. During an async `showModel()` call (triggered by `ChatViewPane.clear()`), the widget can be torn down at an `await` point (`updateWidgetLockState`), leaving `inputPartDisposable.value` as `undefined`. When the await resumes, `setModel()` tries to access `this.inputPart.clearTodoListWidget(...)`, but `inputPart` (backed by the disposed `MutableDisposable`) returns `undefined`, causing the crash.

### Culprit Commit

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Commit | [`d7b18589`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/commit/d7b1858955fdbbce55821c7382782be8e6b96ade) |
| Author | `@justschen` |
| PR | #307689 |
| Message | batched confirmations above the input v1 |
| Why | This commit added `this.inputPart.clearTodoListWidget(model.sessionResource, false)` to `setModel()` (line 1985). Before this commit, the async race condition in `showModel()` existed but was harmless because no code in that region accessed `this.inputPart` on a potentially disposed widget. The new `clearTodoListWidget` call introduced the crash surface. |

### Code Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as User Action
    participant VP as ChatViewPane
    participant W as ChatWidget
    participant MD as MutableDisposable

    User->>VP: clear() / close panel
    VP->>VP: showModel(CancellationToken.None)
    VP->>VP: await updateWidgetLockState()
    Note over VP: Async gap - await in progress
    User->>VP: Panel disposed (e.g. layout change)
    VP->>W: dispose()
    W->>MD: inputPartDisposable.dispose()
    Note over MD: value = undefined
    Note over VP: Await resumes
    VP->>W: setModel(model)
    W->>W: this.inputPart (getter)
    Note over W: inputPartDisposable.value! returns undefined
    W->>W: undefined.clearTodoListWidget()
    Note over W: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined
```

### Affected Files

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/chatWidget.ts` | **Crash site** — `setModel()` at line 1985 accesses `this.inputPart` without disposal guard |
| `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widgetHosts/viewPane/chatViewPane.ts` | **Trigger** — `clear()` calls `showModel(CancellationToken.None)` which cannot be cancelled on disposal |
| `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/input/chatInputPart.ts` | **Data flow** — defines `clearTodoListWidget()` on `ChatInputPart`, accessed via the disposed `inputPartDisposable` |

### Repro Steps

1. Open VS Code Insiders (1.115.0+) with Copilot/Agents enabled
2. Open the Chat view pane and start a session (especially an Agent/Copilot session to trigger `updateWidgetLockState`)
3. Click "New Chat" (triggers `clear()`) and **immediately** close/collapse the Chat panel or switch layouts
4. The race is timing-dependent: the disposal must occur during the `await updateWidgetLockState()` call inside `showModel()`, before `setModel()` is called
5. The error is non-deterministic but more likely on slower machines or when `canResolveChatSession()` takes longer to resolve

### Fix Proposal

**Fix location**: `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/chatWidget.ts` → `setModel()`


2. **Secondary fix** (belt-and-suspenders): In `chatViewPane.ts` `showModel()`, add a disposal check (`if (this._store.isDisposed) return;`) after the `await updateWidgetLockState()` call (line 728) and before `this._widget.setModel(model)` (line 739). This prevents calling into the widget at all when the view pane is disposed.
3. **Optional improvement**: Consider using a cancellable token in `clear()` instead of `CancellationToken.None`, tied to the view pane's lifecycle, so the async operation can be properly cancelled on disposal.

### Recommended Owner

`@justschen` — culprit commit author (PR #307689 "batched confirmations above the input v1"), Microsoft VS Code team member

| [`5cf9162c`](https://errors.code.visualstudio.com/bucket/5cf9162c-66b9-8a2a-c2a5-1895e8cedbe0?product=Agents&commit=98b4d3e24443f6566e694021a376e1760c4a7f62) | Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'clearTodoListWidget') | 1.117.0-insider |

**Impact**: 88 hits, 54 users on 1.117.0-insider (98b4d3e2), all platforms. Error persists across builds — the async race condition in `ChatViewPane.clear()` → `showModel()` → `setModel()` remains unfixed.
